sections.title: 6 Quantitative Signals
sections.content: To complement the qualitative analyses presented above, we examine whether models probability estimates show systematic patterns across reasoning domains. Because this study concerns an ongoing conflict rather than closed historical benchmark, we treat the quantitative labels as an operational measurement choice rather than claim that every question has timeless final answer. For each node-specific verifiable question, we define the ground truth outcome yk {0, 1} based on whether the event had occurred by the papers observation cutoff, and let pk denote the probability estimate manually extracted from the models final response by two researchers following the procedure described in Section 3.3. We give every question at least one-week resolution window, but this should be understood as pragmatic cutoff rather than uniquely privileged one: shorter or longer window could change how some evolving geopolitical events are labeled. We summarize calibration consistency using 1 MAE, where MAE = 1 k=1 pk yk, so that higher values reflect closer agreement between probabilistic judgments and outcomes observed by that cutoff. The aggregated scores are shown in Table 8; detailed per-question results appear in Appendix C, Table 10. (cid:80)N The cross-model average of 0.72 indicates that, despite operating under strict temporal constraints and without access to eventual outcomes, current SOTA LLMs produce probabilistic outputs that broadly align with plausible trajectories of unfolding real-world events. Variation across reasoning domains is more telling than variation across models. Cross-model scores range narrowly from 0.63 to 0.75, and part of this spread reflects differences in response style rather than reasoning quality. Some models tend toward assertive estimates (e.g., 0.95 or 1.00), which can inflate apparent alignment when predictions happen to match outcomes; others produce more conservative estimates that may understate alignment even when the underlying analysis is sound. These stylistic differences make direct model-to-model comparison difficult to interpret. More informative is the pattern across themes. Models found the causal structure of Theme III (Macroeconomic Contagion) most tractable, reaching thematic average of 0.79. The relationship between military disruptions and downstream effects on energy markets and global supply chains appears to admit structurally legible 21 set of causal chains that language models can follow relatively reliably. By contrast, Theme II (Threshold Crossings & Internationalization) and Theme IV (Political Signaling & Regime Dynamics) both average 0.67, suggesting that escalation thresholds, alliance entanglement, and leadership dynamics, which are defined by strategic ambiguity and unstable multi-actor interactions, pose more fundamental challenge. Theme (Initial Outbreak & Military Spillover) occupies middle position at 0.74. Taken together, models appear to be more reliable when tracing structurally legible downstream effects than when interpreting ambiguous strategic intent. We treat these patterns primarily as domain-level map that informs where to look more closely in the qualitative analyses that follow, rather than as scorecard for ranking model capabilities. This caution is especially important here because some questions concern evolving thresholds or partially realized developments, where not observed by the cutoff is analytically useful but not equivalent to saying the event is impossible or permanently resolved. Each question corresponds to concrete situation with its own strategic context, and the central focus of this work remains the reasoning processes and narratives the models produce when confronting an unfolding geopolitical crisis. For this reason, the quantitative results should be read as supportive signals within qualitative case study, not as the primary basis for the papers claims.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We study how large language models reason about an unfolding geopolitical crisis under the fog of war. Using the early stages of the 2026 Middle East conflict as temporally grounded case study, we construct leakage-resistant setting in which models are restricted to information available at each moment, allowing us to examine both their probabilistic judgments and their evolving narratives under genuine uncertainty. Our analysis suggests three main takeaways. First, model responses often show strong strategic reasoning, going beyond surface rhetoric to attend to structural incentives, particularly in settings involving military posture, deterrence, and material constraints. Second, this capability is uneven across domains: models are generally more reliable in economically and logistically structured settings than in politically ambiguous multi-actor environments. Third, their narratives evolve over time, shifting from early expectations of rapid containment toward more systemic accounts of escalation, exhaustion, and fragile de-escalation. Because the conflict remains ongoing at the time of writing, this work captures snapshot of contemporaneous machine reasoning rather than retrospective reconstruction. The quantitative signals reported here should therefore be read as structured indicators anchored to fixed observation cutoff, not as the final closure of every geopolitical question posed in the study. By archiving model responses at each temporal node, we hope to provide useful reference point for future work on temporal reasoning, geopolitical analysis, and AI behavior under real-world uncertainty.

sections.title: A Experiment Settings
sections.content: A.1 Model Access and Identifiers All models are accessed through unified API provider, OpenRouter1, using the OpenAI-compatible chat completions interface. This ensures standardized request format across all evaluated models and eliminates potential discrepancies introduced by provider-specific API behaviors. The exact model identifiers used in our experiments are listed in Table 9. Table 9 Model identifiers used via the OpenRouter API. Model (Paper) kimi-k2.5 qwen3.5-35b-a3b gpt-5.4 claude-sonnet-4.6 gemini-3.1-flash OpenRouter Identifier moonshotai/kimi-k2.5 qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b openai/gpt-5.4 anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6 google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview A.2 Sampling Configuration To promote consistency and reduce stochastic variation in model outputs, we use low temperature setting across all models. The generation parameters are as follows: Temperature: 0.3 Max output tokens: 2,048 Other parameters (top-p, frequency penalty, etc.): default values as defined by each models provider. No system prompt is used. Each query consists solely of single user-role message containing the contextual news corpus followed by the question, as described in Section 3.3. A.3 Single-Run Protocol Each question is posed exactly once to each model. We do not perform multiple runs or aggregate over repeated samples. This design choice reflects our primary goal of studying qualitative reasoning patterns rather than measuring statistical variance. Across 11 temporal nodes, 42 node-specific verifiable questions and 5 general exploratory questions (asked at every node), the study comprises 97 questions per model, yielding total of 485 API calls across 5 evaluated models. A.4 Uniform Context Treatment core design principle of our experiment is that all models receive identical input context for every question at every temporal node. The context construction procedure is as follows: 1. For each temporal node Ti, all articles published strictly before the corresponding event timestamp are collected from 12 international news sources. 2. Articles are sorted in reverse chronological order (newest first). 3. Each article is formatted as: [Date] Title (Source), followed by the article body text truncated to maximum of 2,000 characters. Articles whose body text is identical to their title are included as headline-only entries to conserve token budget. 1https://openrouter.ai/ 26 4. Articles are concatenated sequentially until the total context reaches hard cap of 480,000 characters (approximately 120,000 tokens). truncation notice ([...N older articles omitted for brevity]) is appended when the budget is exceeded. 5. The question and instruction are appended after the context, following the prompt structure described in Section 3.3. A.5 Handling Heterogeneous Context Windows The evaluated models have different maximum context lengths: gpt-5.4, claude-sonnet-4.6, and gemini-3.1-flash each support approximately 1M tokens, while qwen3.5-35b-a3b and kimi-k2.5 each support 256K tokens. Rather than tailoring input length to each models capacity, we apply uniform context budget of 480,000 characters (120K tokens) to all models. This budget was set conservatively to remain well within the context window of every model, including the smallest (256K tokens), while leaving room for the 2,048 output tokens. In practice, the actual prompt length grows from 244K characters (61K tokens) at T0 (158 articles) to the 480K-character ceiling at T10 (1,787 articles). Because even the longest prompts stay within all models supported windows, no model-specific truncation or summarization is applied, ensuring that observed differences in outputs are attributable to reasoning capabilities rather than information asymmetry. A.6 API Reliability To ensure robustness against transient API failures, each call is wrapped with retry mechanism: up to 3 attempts with exponential backoff (base 2 seconds), and minimum 10-second wait for rate-limit responses. 1.5-second delay is inserted between consecutive calls to the same model to respect rate limits. Models are queried in parallel (one thread per model), while questions within each model are processed sequentially to preserve temporal ordering in logging.

sections.title: B Complete Examples
sections.content: For better understanding of the models reasoning trajectories, we provide the complete examples for the question Is Iran likely to retaliate militarily in response to the strikes? at the second temporal node (T1: Feb 28, Israeli-US Strikes). Other responses are available in the released dataset. For better readability, we transfer the responses in original markdown format to LaTeX format. kimi-k2.5: Based on the current intelligence and public statements from both sides, yes, Iran is highly likely to retaliate militarily if the United States conducts strikes on Iranian territory. The probability of Iranian military retaliation in response to US strikes is approximately 85-90%. Analysis of Key Factors 1. Explicit Iranian Commitments to Retaliation Iranian leadership has issued unambiguous warnings that any US attackregardless of scalewill trigger military response. Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei stated: There is no such thing as limited attack. An act of aggression is an act of aggression... Irans response to any such attack would be firm and severe. Supreme Leader Ayatollah Khamenei has similarly warned that any US strike would spark regional war and that all US bases, facilities, and assets in the Middle East would constitute legitimate targets. 2. Historical Precedent During the June 2025 12-Day War, Iran retaliated against US forces even when significantly weakened, launching ballistic missiles at the Al Udeid Air Base in Qatar (though with advance warning to minimize casualties). Despite suffering severe degradation to its air defenses and nuclear facilities in that conflict, Tehran demonstrated doctrinal commitment to direct retaliation rather than absorbing strikes passively. 3. Current Military Posture and Capabilities Despite post-war degradation, Iran has spent the intervening months rebuilding missile production facilities and fortifying nuclear sites. The IRGC has conducted live-fire drills in the Strait of Hormuzthrough 27 which 20% of global oil flowssignaling readiness to disrupt global energy markets. Iran possesses significant shortand medium-range ballistic missile inventories (estimated 2,000-3,000 km range) capable of targeting US installations in Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, the UAE, and Iraq. 4. Proxy Network Activation While weakened, Iran retains the ability to activate proxy forces including Hezbollah in Lebanon, the Houthis in Yemen, and militias in Iraq. Lebanese officials have already warned that Israel could strike Beiruts airport if Hezbollah enters US-Iran conflict on Tehrans behalf, suggesting contingency planning for proxy escalation is underway. 5. Strategic Calculus Iranian leaders likely view restraint in the face of US strikes as existential weakness that could encourage further attacks or regime change efforts. With the regime already facing severe domestic pressure following the violent suppression of protests (7,000+ killed), leadership cannot afford to appear weak or submissive to American military pressure without risking internal collapse. Constraints and Limitations While retaliation is highly probable, its scale may be calibrated. Iran may seek to avoid total war that could lead to regime collapse, potentially opting for proportionate strikes on US bases combined with asymmetric attacks via proxies rather than all-out regional warfare. However, the articles indicate Iranian officials reject the concept of limited responses, suggesting any retaliation could rapidly escalate beyond controlled parameters. Probability Assessment: 85-90% qwen3.5-35b-a3b: Heres thinking process that leads to the suggested analysis: 1. Analyze the Request: Source Material: large collection of news snippets (simulated future news from 2026) regarding tensions between the US, Iran, Israel, and other regional actors. Task: Analyze the current situation and potential future direction based only on the provided text. Specific Question: Is Iran likely to retaliate militarily in response to the strikes? Output Requirement: Provide the analysis and end with probability score. 2. Scan and Synthesize the Provided Text: Context: The date is February 2026. There is history of conflict: 12-day war occurred in June 2025 between Israel and Iran (with US involvement). Protests in Iran were crushed in January 2026 (thousands killed). Current Status: US and Iran are in nuclear talks (Geneva, Oman, Vienna). Mediators include Oman. Trump is President (second term). Military buildup is massive (USS Gerald R. Ford, USS Abraham Lincoln, E-3 Sentry planes, etc.). Irans Stance: Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi: There is no such thing as limited attack. An act of aggression is an act of aggression. (Feb 27). Supreme Leader Khamenei: If they start war this time, it will be regional war. (Feb 1). IRGC: We are ready for any option in war conditions. (Feb 5). Threats: Severe response even to limited strikes (Feb 23). All bases, facilities and assets... would constitute legitimate targets (Feb 19). Actions: Military drills in Strait of Hormuz (Feb 17, 18). Building concrete shields at nuclear sites (Feb 19). Loading oil onto ships in anticipation of strikes (Feb 25). Diplomacy: Willing to talk (encouraging signals, deal achievable) but insists on nuclear-only focus, rejecting missile/proxy discussions (Feb 26). US/Israel Stance: Trump: Considering limited military strike, 10-15 days deadline (Feb 20, 23). Bad things happen if no deal (Feb 19). Military: Highly kinetic campaign ready (Feb 22). Weeks-long operations possible (Feb 15). 28 Israel: Preparing for war, army chief silent publicly but warns of war of attrition (Feb 26). Regional Context: Pakistan/Afghanistan war (Feb 27). UK pulling diplomats from Iran (Feb 27). US evacuating non-essential staff from Jerusalem (Feb 27). Maersk diverting ships (Feb 27). Oil prices fluctuating based on war fears (Feb 13, 24). 3. Evaluate the Specific Question: Is Iran likely to retaliate militarily in response to the strikes? Evidence FOR Retaliation: Explicit threats from top leadership (Khamenei, Araghchi, IRGC). Doctrine: No such thing as limited attack. Capability: Rebuilt missile facilities (Feb 19), new weapons tests (Feb 24), drills in Hormuz (Feb 17). Precedent: Retaliated in June 2025 (limited strike on Qatar base). Strategic Logic: To maintain deterrence and regime survival, they must respond to aggression. Current Posture: Ready for any scenario (Feb 5). Evidence AGAINST Retaliation (or mitigating factors): Desire for diplomatic deal (Araghchi says deal achievable, prepared for peace). Fear of regime collapse (internal protests crushed, economy struggling). US military buildup is overwhelming (Ford, Lincoln, etc.). Some sources suggest Iran might try to avoid war if deal is reached (Feb 26). However, the question asks about retaliation in response to strikes. If strikes happen, the diplomatic window closes. Synthesis: If the US/Israel strikes occur (which the text suggests is highly likely given the 90% chance cited by sources in Feb 18), Iran has explicitly stated they will respond. The text emphasizes that Iran views any attack as an act of aggression requiring decisive and proportionate response (Feb 19). The text also notes that Iran has rebuilt capabilities specifically for this contingency. While they prefer diplomacy, the threshold for retaliation is set very low (limited attack = severe response). 4. Draft the Analysis: Current Situation: High tension, military buildup, nuclear talks ongoing but failing to bridge gaps (enrichment vs. zero enrichment). Irans Intent: Explicitly stated they will retaliate. Severe response even to limited strikes. Capabilities: Rebuilt missile bases, drills in Hormuz, concrete shields. Constraints: Internal instability (protests), economic strain, fear of total war. Conclusion: If strikes happen, retaliation is highly probable based on stated doctrine and capability. 5. Determine Probability: The text is filled with statements from Iranian officials confirming retaliation is the plan if attacked. Irans response to US attacks will be severe even if President Donald Trump orders only limited military action (Feb 23). All bases... would constitute legitimate targets (Feb 19). We are ready for any option in war conditions (Feb 5). Given the explicit nature of these statements and the military preparations, the probability of some form of military retaliation (missiles, drones, proxies) is very high if the strikes occur. Probability: High (85-95%). will select specific number based on the weight of the evidence. The text says 90% chance we see kinetic action regarding the US strike, and Irans response is framed as certainty in their own rhetoric. Lets estimate 90% for retaliation if strikes occur. 6. Refine the Output: Structure: Situation Analysis -> Future Direction -> Answer to Question -> Probability. Ensure all claims are backed by the provided text snippets (e.g., citing dates and sources like Al-Monitor, The National, etc.). 29 Self-Correction on Date: The text is clearly set in the future (2026). must treat this as the current reality for the analysis, not as fiction. Check: Did answer the specific question? Yes. Did provide probability? Yes. 7. Final Review of the Text for Nuance: There is tension between wants deal and will fight. Text: Iran is ready to strike deal with the US to avoid the gamble of war (Feb 24). Text: Irans response to US attacks will be severe (Feb 23). The question asks about retaliation in response to strikes. This implies the strikes have happened or are imminent. If the strikes happen, the deal is off. Therefore, the wants deal aspect becomes secondary to the will fight aspect. The text explicitly mentions Iran has rebuilt damaged missile facilities (Feb 19) and is fortifying nuclear sites (Feb 19). The text mentions Iran has threatened retaliation against any strike (Feb 23). Therefore, the likelihood is extremely high. 8. Final Probability Decision: 90%. (High confidence based on explicit statements). (Self-Correction during drafting): Wait, need to make sure dont hallucinate. must stick strictly to the provided text. Snippet: Irans response to US attacks will be severe even if President Donald Trump orders only limited military action (Feb 23). Snippet: All bases, facilities and assets of the hostile force in the region would constitute legitimate targets (Feb 19). Snippet: We are ready for any option in war conditions (Feb 5). Snippet: Iran vows retaliation will be severe (Feb 23). Snippet: Irans army says it is ready for war (Feb 5). Snippet: Iran is ready to strike deal... to avoid the gamble of war gpt-5.4: Yesif the US and/or Israel conduct meaningful strikes on Iran, Iran is likely to retaliate militarily. Why thats the base case: Iranian officials have repeatedly and publicly said any attack would be met with firm, severe, decisive and proportionate response. Iran has signaled that US bases and assets in the region would be considered legitimate targets. Tehran appears to believe failing to respond would weaken deterrence, invite further attacks, and damage regime credibility at home and with regional partners. Regional military movesUS force buildup, embassy drawdowns, dispersal of ships, coalition repositioning in Iraq, Israeli preparations, shipping warningsstrongly suggest policymakers expect some form of Iranian response, not none. That said, the more important question is not whether Iran retaliates, but how much. Most likely forms of retaliation: 1. Missile/drone strikes on US bases in the Gulf or Iraq/Syria This is the most direct and visible option. Iran has capability here, though effectiveness depends on surviving launch capacity and air defenses. 2. Strikes on Israel Especially if Israel participates directly or is seen as enabling the operation. 3. Proxy or partner attacks Iraq/Syria militias, Houthis, or other aligned actors could hit US, Israeli, or shipping targets. Even if Irans proxy network is weaker than before, it still gives Tehran deniable options. 4. Maritime disruption Harassment in the Gulf/Strait of Hormuz, mining risk, anti-ship missiles, drones, seizures, or limited traffic disruption. Full closure is less likely because it would be highly escalatory and economically costly for Iran too. 30 5. Cyber operations Likely as supplement, not substitute. What may limit retaliation: Iran was weakened by the 2025 war and may want to avoid regime-threatening escalation. Tehran may calibrate its response to avoid full-scale US campaign. If strikes are narrow and clearly one-off, Iran could choose limited, symbolic but real retaliation. Ongoing diplomacy gives Iran some incentive to preserve room for de-escalation.
